title: Senegal appeal date set after Afcon win overturned
dek: The Court of Arbitration for Sport has scheduled October 8, 2026 for a hearing on Senegal's appeal against CAF's decision to strip them of the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations title. Senegal won the final 1-0 against Morocco after extra time on January 18, but CAF's appeal board ruled them forfeited after they left the pitch in protest of a penalty award deep into stoppage time, awarding Morocco a 3-0 win on March 17.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: CAS has set October 8, 2026 as the hearing date for Senegal's appeal against CAF's title stripping decision, per ESPN and BBC reporting.
bottom_line: October 8 marks the first full hearing on whether a team that won a continental final on the pitch can have that title overturned by federation disciplinary action. CAS's decision will reshape how African football governs protest and referee review disputes at the highest level.
body: The Court of Arbitration for Sport has scheduled October 8, 2026 for a hearing on Senegal's appeal against the Confederation of African Football's decision to strip them of the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations title, per ESPN's reporting. The decision sets a date for sport's highest court to review one of African football's most contested rulings: a tournament title won on the pitch but revoked on disciplinary grounds.

Senegal defeated Morocco 1-0 after extra time in the final on January 18, with Pape Gueye scoring the winner. The match turned in stoppage time when a penalty was awarded to Morocco following a VAR review. The score was 0-0 at that moment. Senegal's then-coach Pape Thiaw led his players off the pitch in protest of the penalty award. After they returned, Morocco's Brahim Díaz missed the penalty.

CAF's appeal board ruled on March 17 that Senegal had forfeited the final and awarded Morocco a 3-0 victory. Senegal confirmed their intention to appeal to CAS that same month. The parties involved, the Senegalese federation, CAF, and the Moroccan federation, did not agree to expedite the procedure, meaning the case will follow a standard timeline, according to ESPN.

The CAS hearing will take place behind closed doors, and a decision will not be issued on the day of the hearing itself. The proceedings will test whether CAF's authority to overturn match results on disciplinary grounds survives international scrutiny, and what legal standard applies when a team protests a refereeing decision by walking off the pitch.

The three open questions remain unaddressed by the sources: the specific grounds of Senegal's appeal and CAF's legal defense, the timeline for CAS's decision after October 8, and whether Senegal could be awarded the title or whether other remedies are available under CAS jurisdiction.
